'''National Historical Science Movement''' (国民的歴史学運動, Kokuminteki Rekishigaku Undō) refers to a series of [[:en:Social movement|social movements]] undertaken in the field of {{ill|historical science|ja|歴史学|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/historical science|qq=}} and {{ill|mass movements|ja|大衆運動|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mass movements|qq=}} by the History Division of the {{ill|Democratic Scientists Association|ja|民主主義科学者協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Democratic Scientists Association|qq=}} (Minka) from the early to mid-{{ill|1950s|ja|1950th year 代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1950s|qq=}}.<ref name="村井1985">{{Cite journal|和書|author=村井淳志 |date=1985-07 |title=国民的歴史学運動と歴史教育 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10748/2598 |journal=教育科学研究 |ISSN=0289-7121 |publisher=東京都立大学教育学研究室 |volume=4 |pages=23-32}}</ref> The movement was proposed by historian [[:en:Ishimoda Shō|Masao Ishimoto]], who was researching {{ill|Japanese medieval history|ja|日本中世史|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese medieval history|qq=}}, shortly after Minka declared its goal of creating and disseminating "national science" in [[:en:1952|1952]].<ref name="村井1985" /> Based on slogans like "revolution in historical science" and "making historical science belong to the people," the movement aligned with the armed struggle policy of the [[:en:Japanese Communist Party|Japanese Communist Party]], particularly the {{ill|Shokan faction|ja|所感派|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokan faction|qq=}}.<ref name="nation">小熊 2002nd year p.325</ref>
The blending of [[:en:Ethnic nationalism|nationalism]] and {{ill|politicism|ja|政治主義|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/politicism|qq=}} in the movement sparked intense debate. Following the {{ill|Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|ja|日本共産党第6回全国協議会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|qq=}} in 1955, which marked a policy shift, and the dissolution of Minka's History Division between 1955 and 1956, the movement began to wane.<ref name="村井1985" /> Ishimoto himself submitted a self-critical essay in 1957, and the National Historical Science Movement eventually became regarded as a "nightmare of the past" in the field of historical science.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" /> Meanwhile, [[:en:Positivism|positivism]] based on written historical sources gained prominence in Japanese historical studies.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" />
== History == === Democratic Scientists Association === The Democratic Scientists Association (Minka) was established in [[:en:1946|1946]] with 180 members.<ref name="邱静">{{Cite thesis|和書 |author=邱静 |title=戦後における知識人の思想と政治 : 憲法問題研究会を中心に |volume=早稲田大学 |series=博士 (学術) 甲第2555号 |year=2008 |naid=500000435149 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2065/28633}}</ref> The association was critical of the prewar attitudes of scientists toward [[:en:Power (social and political)|authority]], the hierarchical structure of academic societies, and the lack of openness in science.<ref name="村井1985" />
Minka advocated for a new form of science, referred to comprehensively as "democratic science," and pursued research along these lines.<ref name="村井1985" /> By [[:en:1950|1950]], it had reached its peak with 114 local branches, 1,772 specialized members, and 8,243 ordinary members, including researchers, general citizens, and students.<ref name="邱静" /> During the {{ill|US occupation of Japan|ja|アメリカ軍占領期|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/US occupation of Japan|qq=}}, Minka exerted significant influence on academia and public discourse.<ref name="邱静" />
=== Transition from "Democratic Science" to "National Science" === Amid the surge of [[:en:Nationalism|nationalism]] across Asia after [[:en:World War II|World War II]], Minka adopted a new policy in its sixth congress (May 1951), declaring the need for "science that serves and learns from the people." By its seventh congress (May 1952), it aimed for the "creation and dissemination of national science."<ref name="村井1985" />
Ishimoto first introduced the term "national science" in January 1952, asserting the necessity of creating a science that could counter US oppression and contribute to {{ill|national liberation|ja|民族解放|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/national liberation|qq=}}.<ref name="村井1985" />
==== National Science and the National Historical Science Movement ==== The National Historical Science Movement arose under the broader slogan of "creating and disseminating national science," emphasizing the need for historians to engage with the public and create a new type of historical science based on [[:en:Marxism|Marxism]].<ref name="yoshioka">[[佐々木毅]]他編『[[戦後史大辞典]] 増補縮刷版』[[三省堂]]、1995th year 6月、p.296</ref>

[[sn:National History Movement]] [[ja:国民的歴史学運動]]
<!--National History Movement--> [[Category:戦後日本の社会運動]] [[Category:Historiography of Japan]] [[Category:日本の歴史学]] [[Category:Cold War history of Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->

'''National Historical Science Movement''' (国民的歴史学運動, Kokuminteki Rekishigaku Undō) refers to a series of [[:en:Social movement|social movements]] undertaken in the field of {{ill|historical science|ja|歴史学|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/historical science|qq=}} and {{ill|mass movements|ja|大衆運動|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mass movements|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 市民活動}} by the History Division of the {{ill|Democratic Scientists Association|ja|民主主義科学者協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Democratic Scientists Association|qq=}} (Minka) from the early to mid-{{ill|1950s|ja|1950th year 代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1950s|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="村井1985">{{Cite journal|和書|author=村井淳志 |date=1985-07 |title=国民的歴史学運動と歴史教育 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10748/2598 |journal=教育科学研究 |ISSN=0289-7121 |publisher=東京都立大学教育学研究室 |volume=4 |pages=23-32}}</ref> The movement was proposed by historian [[:en:Ishimoda Shō|Masao Ishimoto]], who was researching {{ill|Japanese medieval history|ja|日本中世史|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese medieval history|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中世}}, shortly after Minka declared its goal of creating and disseminating "national science" in [[:en:1952|1952]].<ref name="村井1985" /> Based on slogans like "revolution in historical science" and "making historical science belong to the people," the movement aligned with the armed struggle policy of the [[:en:Japanese Communist Party|Japanese Communist Party]], particularly the {{ill|Shokan faction|ja|所感派|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokan faction|qq=}}.<ref name="nation">小熊 2002nd year p.325</ref>
The blending of [[:en:Ethnic nationalism|nationalism]] and {{ill|politicism|ja|政治主義|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/politicism|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the movement sparked intense debate. Following the {{ill|Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|ja|日本共産党第6回全国協議会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|qq=}} in 1955, which marked a policy shift, and the dissolution of Minka's History Division between 1955 and 1956, the movement began to wane.<ref name="村井1985" /> Ishimoto himself submitted a self-critical essay in 1957, and the National Historical Science Movement eventually became regarded as a "nightmare of the past" in the field of historical science.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" /> Meanwhile, [[:en:Positivism|positivism]] based on written historical sources gained prominence in Japanese historical studies.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" />
== History == === Democratic Scientists Association === The Democratic Scientists Association (Minka) was established in [[:en:1946|1946]] with 180 members.<ref name="邱静">{{Cite thesis|和書 |author=邱静 |title=戦後における知識人の思想と政治 : 憲法問題研究会を中心に |volume=早稲田大学 |series=博士 (学術) 甲第2555号 |year=2008 |naid=500000435149 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2065/28633}}</ref> The association was critical of the prewar attitudes of scientists toward [[:en:Power (social and political)|authority]], the hierarchical structure of academic societies, and the lack of openness in science.<ref name="村井1985" />
Minka advocated for a new form of science, referred to comprehensively as "democratic science," and pursued research along these lines.<ref name="村井1985" /> By [[:en:1950|1950]], it had reached its peak with 114 local branches, 1,772 specialized members, and 8,243 ordinary members, including researchers, general citizens, and students.<ref name="邱静" /> During the {{ill|US occupation of Japan|ja|アメリカ軍占領期|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/US occupation of Japan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Minka exerted significant influence on academia and public discourse.<ref name="邱静" />
=== Transition from "Democratic Science" to "National Science" === Amid the surge of [[:en:Nationalism|nationalism]] across Asia after [[:en:World War II|World War II]], Minka adopted a new policy in its sixth congress (May 1951), declaring the need for "science that serves and learns from the people." By its seventh congress (May 1952), it aimed for the "creation and dissemination of national science."<ref name="村井1985" />
Ishimoto first introduced the term "national science" in January 1952, asserting the necessity of creating a science that could counter US oppression and contribute to {{ill|national liberation|ja|民族解放|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/national liberation|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="村井1985" />
==== National Science and the National Historical Science Movement ==== The National Historical Science Movement arose under the broader slogan of "creating and disseminating national science," emphasizing the need for historians to engage with the public and create a new type of historical science based on [[:en:Marxism|Marxism]].<ref name="yoshioka">[[佐々木毅]]他編『[[戦後史大辞典]] 増補縮刷版』[[三省堂]]、1995th year 6月、p.296</ref>
=== Decline of the Movement === From 1955 to 1956, Minka's History Division disbanded, and the movement's organizational basis collapsed.<ref name="村井1985" /> Internal disputes within the Japanese Communist Party and external criticism of the movement's methodology accelerated its decline.<ref name="oguma3">小熊 2002nd year p.331</ref>
== Legacy == The National Historical Science Movement had a lasting impact on the study of history in Japan, particularly in highlighting the importance of involving ordinary people in historical narrative construction.
=== Women's History === The focus on oral histories and the inclusion of women in historical narratives laid the groundwork for the emergence of [[:en:Women's history|women's history]] as a new field of study.<ref name="oohara">{{Cite journal|和書|author=倉敷伸子 |date=2007-11 |title=女性史研究とオーラル・ヒストリー |journal=大原社会問題研究所雑誌 |ISSN=09129421 |publisher=法政大学大原社会問題研究所 |issue=588 |pages=15-27 |id={{NDLJP|9972363}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/9972363/1/1}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Controversies === The movement has been both praised for its democratic ideals and criticized for its politicization and methodological weaknesses. Scholars such as [[:en:Eiji Oguma|Eiji Oguma]] have assessed the movement as a necessary yet flawed attempt to democratize historical science.<ref name="amino">『網野善彦対談集 「日本」をめぐって』、[[講談社]]、2002nd year 1月、p.217 - 219</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Ishimoda Shō|Masao Ishimoto]] * {{ill|Democratic Scientists Association|ja|民主主義科学者協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Democratic Scientists Association|qq=}} * [[:en:Nationalism|Nationalism]]
{{translated page|ja|国民的歴史学運動|version=103249691|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Following the {{ill|Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|ja|日本共産党第6回全国協議会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|qq=}} in 1955, which marked a policy shift, and the dissolution of Minka's History Division between 1955 and 1956, the movement began to wane.<ref name="村井1985" /> Ishimoto himself submitted a self-critical essay in 1957, and the National Historical Science Movement eventually became regarded as a "nightmare of the past" in the field of historical science.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" /> Meanwhile, [[:en:Positivism|positivism]] based on written historical sources gained prominence in Japanese historical studies.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" />

Minka advocated for a new form of science, referred to comprehensively as "democratic science," and pursued research along these lines.<ref name="村井1985" /> By [[:en:1950|1950]], it had reached its peak with 114 local branches, 1,772 specialized members, and 8,243 ordinary members, including researchers, general citizens, and students.<ref name="邱静" /> During the {{ill|US occupation of Japan|ja|アメリカ軍占領期|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/US occupation of Japan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Minka exerted significant influence on academia and public discourse.<ref name="邱静" />
=== Transition from "Democratic Science" to "National Science" === Amid the surge of [[:en:Nationalism|nationalism]] across Asia after [[:en:World War II|World War II]], Minka adopted a new policy in its sixth congress (May 1951), declaring the need for "science that serves and learns from the people." By its seventh congress (May 1952), it aimed for the "creation and dissemination of national science."<ref name="村井1985" />
Ishimoto first introduced the term "national science" in January 1952, asserting the necessity of creating a science that could counter US oppression and contribute to {{ill|national liberation|ja|民族解放|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/national liberation|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="村井1985" />
==== National Science and the National Historical Science Movement ==== The National Historical Science Movement arose under the broader slogan of "creating and disseminating national science," emphasizing the need for historians to engage with the public and create a new type of historical science based on [[:en:Marxism|Marxism]].<ref name="yoshioka">[[佐々木毅]]他編『[[戦後史大辞典]] 増補縮刷版』[[:en:三省堂|三省堂]]、1995th year 6月、p.296</ref>
=== Decline of the Movement === From 1955 to 1956, Minka's History Division disbanded, and the movement's organizational basis collapsed.<ref name="村井1985" /> Internal disputes within the Japanese Communist Party and external criticism of the movement's methodology accelerated its decline.<ref name="oguma3">小熊 2002nd year p.331</ref>
== Legacy == The National Historical Science Movement had a lasting impact on the study of history in Japan, particularly in highlighting the importance of involving ordinary people in historical narrative construction.
=== Women's History === The focus on oral histories and the inclusion of women in historical narratives laid the groundwork for the emergence of [[:en:Women's history|women's history]] as a new field of study.<ref name="oohara">{{Cite journal|和書|author=倉敷伸子 |date=2007-11 |title=女性史研究とオーラル・ヒストリー |journal=大原社会問題研究所雑誌 |ISSN=09129421 |publisher=法政大学大原社会問題研究所 |issue=588 |pages=15-27 |id={{NDLJP|9972363}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/9972363/1/1}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Controversies === The movement has been both praised for its democratic ideals and criticized for its politicization and methodological weaknesses. Scholars such as [[:en:Eiji Oguma|Eiji Oguma]] have assessed the movement as a necessary yet flawed attempt to democratize historical science.<ref name="amino">『網野善彦対談集 「日本」をめぐって』、[[:en:講談社|講談社]]、2002nd year 1月、p.217 - 219</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Ishimoda Shō|Masao Ishimoto]] * {{ill|Democratic Scientists Association|ja|民主主義科学者協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Democratic Scientists Association|qq=}} * [[:en:Nationalism|Nationalism]]
{{translated page|ja|国民的歴史学運動|version=103249691|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Completion tier 3]]

revision.comment: Merge [[Category:日本の歴史学]] into [[Category:Historiography of Japan]] via bot
revision.text:
'''National Historical Science Movement''' (国民的歴史学運動, Kokuminteki Rekishigaku Undō) refers to a series of [[:en:Social movement|social movements]] undertaken in the field of {{ill|historical science|ja|歴史学|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/historical science|qq=}} and {{ill|mass movements|ja|大衆運動|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mass movements|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 市民活動}} by the History Division of the {{ill|Democratic Scientists Association|ja|民主主義科学者協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Democratic Scientists Association|qq=}} (Minka) from the early to mid-{{ill|1950s|ja|1950th year 代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1950s|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="村井1985">{{Cite journal|和書|author=村井淳志 |date=1985-07 |title=国民的歴史学運動と歴史教育 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10748/2598 |journal=教育科学研究 |ISSN=0289-7121 |publisher=東京都立大学教育学研究室 |volume=4 |pages=23-32}}</ref> The movement was proposed by historian [[:en:Ishimoda Shō|Masao Ishimoto]], who was researching {{ill|Japanese medieval history|ja|日本中世史|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese medieval history|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中世}}, shortly after Minka declared its goal of creating and disseminating "national science" in [[:en:1952|1952]].<ref name="村井1985" /> Based on slogans like "revolution in historical science" and "making historical science belong to the people," the movement aligned with the armed struggle policy of the [[:en:Japanese Communist Party|Japanese Communist Party]], particularly the {{ill|Shokan faction|ja|所感派|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokan faction|qq=}}.<ref name="nation">小熊 2002nd year p.325</ref>
The blending of [[:en:Ethnic nationalism|nationalism]] and {{ill|politicism|ja|政治主義|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/politicism|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the movement sparked intense debate. Following the {{ill|Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|ja|日本共産党第6回全国協議会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|qq=}} in 1955, which marked a policy shift, and the dissolution of Minka's History Division between 1955 and 1956, the movement began to wane.<ref name="村井1985" /> Ishimoto himself submitted a self-critical essay in 1957, and the National Historical Science Movement eventually became regarded as a "nightmare of the past" in the field of historical science.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" /> Meanwhile, [[:en:Positivism|positivism]] based on written historical sources gained prominence in Japanese historical studies.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" />
== History == === Democratic Scientists Association === The Democratic Scientists Association (Minka) was established in [[:en:1946|1946]] with 180 members.<ref name="邱静">{{Cite thesis|和書 |author=邱静 |title=戦後における知識人の思想と政治 : 憲法問題研究会を中心に |volume=早稲田大学 |series=博士 (学術) 甲第2555号 |year=2008 |naid=500000435149 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2065/28633}}</ref> The association was critical of the prewar attitudes of scientists toward [[:en:Power (social and political)|authority]], the hierarchical structure of academic societies, and the lack of openness in science.<ref name="村井1985" />
Minka advocated for a new form of science, referred to comprehensively as "democratic science," and pursued research along these lines.<ref name="村井1985" /> By [[:en:1950|1950]], it had reached its peak with 114 local branches, 1,772 specialized members, and 8,243 ordinary members, including researchers, general citizens, and students.<ref name="邱静" /> During the {{ill|US occupation of Japan|ja|アメリカ軍占領期|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/US occupation of Japan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Minka exerted significant influence on academia and public discourse.<ref name="邱静" />
=== Transition from "Democratic Science" to "National Science" === Amid the surge of [[:en:Nationalism|nationalism]] across Asia after [[:en:World War II|World War II]], Minka adopted a new policy in its sixth congress (May 1951), declaring the need for "science that serves and learns from the people." By its seventh congress (May 1952), it aimed for the "creation and dissemination of national science."<ref name="村井1985" />
Ishimoto first introduced the term "national science" in January 1952, asserting the necessity of creating a science that could counter US oppression and contribute to {{ill|national liberation|ja|民族解放|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/national liberation|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="村井1985" />
==== National Science and the National Historical Science Movement ==== The National Historical Science Movement arose under the broader slogan of "creating and disseminating national science," emphasizing the need for historians to engage with the public and create a new type of historical science based on [[:en:Marxism|Marxism]].<ref name="yoshioka">[[佐々木毅]]他編『[[戦後史大辞典]] 増補縮刷版』[[:en:三省堂|三省堂]]、1995th year 6月、p.296</ref>
=== Decline of the Movement === From 1955 to 1956, Minka's History Division disbanded, and the movement's organizational basis collapsed.<ref name="村井1985" /> Internal disputes within the Japanese Communist Party and external criticism of the movement's methodology accelerated its decline.<ref name="oguma3">小熊 2002nd year p.331</ref>
== Legacy == The National Historical Science Movement had a lasting impact on the study of history in Japan, particularly in highlighting the importance of involving ordinary people in historical narrative construction.
=== Women's History === The focus on oral histories and the inclusion of women in historical narratives laid the groundwork for the emergence of [[:en:Women's history|women's history]] as a new field of study.<ref name="oohara">{{Cite journal|和書|author=倉敷伸子 |date=2007-11 |title=女性史研究とオーラル・ヒストリー |journal=大原社会問題研究所雑誌 |ISSN=09129421 |publisher=法政大学大原社会問題研究所 |issue=588 |pages=15-27 |id={{NDLJP|9972363}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/9972363/1/1}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Controversies === The movement has been both praised for its democratic ideals and criticized for its politicization and methodological weaknesses. Scholars such as [[:en:Eiji Oguma|Eiji Oguma]] have assessed the movement as a necessary yet flawed attempt to democratize historical science.<ref name="amino">『網野善彦対談集 「日本」をめぐって』、[[:en:講談社|講談社]]、2002nd year 1月、p.217 - 219</ref>

=== Women's History === The focus on oral histories and the inclusion of women in historical narratives laid the groundwork for the emergence of [[:en:Women's history|women's history]] as a new field of study.<ref name="oohara">{{Cite journal|和書|author=倉敷伸子 |date=2007-11 |title=女性史研究とオーラル・ヒストリー |journal=大原社会問題研究所雑誌 |ISSN=09129421 |publisher=法政大学大原社会問題研究所 |issue=588 |pages=15-27 |id={{NDLJP|9972363}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/9972363/1/1}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Controversies === The movement has been both praised for its democratic ideals and criticized for its politicization and methodological weaknesses. Scholars such as [[:en:Eiji Oguma|Eiji Oguma]] have assessed the movement as a necessary yet flawed attempt to democratize historical science.<ref name="amino">『網野善彦対談集 「日本」をめぐって』、[[:en:講談社|講談社]]、2002nd year 1月、p.217 - 219</ref>

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q17228488]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''National Historical Science Movement''' (国民的歴史学運動, Kokuminteki Rekishigaku Undō) refers to a series of [[:en:Social movement|social movements]] undertaken in the field of {{ill|historical science|ja|歴史学|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/historical science|qq=|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} and {{ill|mass movements|ja|大衆運動|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mass movements|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 市民活動|1=civic activity|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17173040}} by the History Division of the {{ill|Democratic Scientists Association|ja|民主主義科学者協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Democratic Scientists Association|qq=|1=Association of Democratic Scientists|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547833}} (Minka) from the early to mid-{{ill|1950s|ja|1950th year 代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1950s|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="村井1985">{{Cite journal|和書|author=村井淳志 |date=1985-07 |title=国民的歴史学運動と歴史教育 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10748/2598 |journal=教育科学研究 |ISSN=0289-7121 |publisher=東京都立大学教育学研究室 |volume=4 |pages=23-32}}</ref> The movement was proposed by historian [[:en:Ishimoda Shō|Masao Ishimoto]], who was researching {{ill|Japanese medieval history|ja|日本中世史|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese medieval history|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中世|1=Middle Ages|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q12554}}, shortly after Minka declared its goal of creating and disseminating "national science" in [[:en:1952|1952]].<ref name="村井1985" /> Based on slogans like "revolution in historical science" and "making historical science belong to the people," the movement aligned with the armed struggle policy of the [[:en:Japanese Communist Party|Japanese Communist Party]], particularly the {{ill|Shokan faction|ja|所感派|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokan faction|qq=|1=Shokan sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11496759}}.<ref name="nation">小熊 2002nd year p.325</ref>
The blending of [[:en:Ethnic nationalism|nationalism]] and {{ill|politicism|ja|政治主義|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/politicism|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the movement sparked intense debate. Following the {{ill|Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|ja|日本共産党第6回全国協議会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|qq=|1=Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11507340}} in 1955, which marked a policy shift, and the dissolution of Minka's History Division between 1955 and 1956, the movement began to wane.<ref name="村井1985" /> Ishimoto himself submitted a self-critical essay in 1957, and the National Historical Science Movement eventually became regarded as a "nightmare of the past" in the field of historical science.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" /> Meanwhile, [[:en:Positivism|positivism]] based on written historical sources gained prominence in Japanese historical studies.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" />
== History == === Democratic Scientists Association === The Democratic Scientists Association (Minka) was established in [[:en:1946|1946]] with 180 members.<ref name="邱静">{{Cite thesis|和書 |author=邱静 |title=戦後における知識人の思想と政治 : 憲法問題研究会を中心に |volume=早稲田大学 |series=博士 (学術) 甲第2555号 |year=2008 |naid=500000435149 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2065/28633}}</ref> The association was critical of the prewar attitudes of scientists toward [[:en:Power (social and political)|authority]], the hierarchical structure of academic societies, and the lack of openness in science.<ref name="村井1985" />
Minka advocated for a new form of science, referred to comprehensively as "democratic science," and pursued research along these lines.<ref name="村井1985" /> By [[:en:1950|1950]], it had reached its peak with 114 local branches, 1,772 specialized members, and 8,243 ordinary members, including researchers, general citizens, and students.<ref name="邱静" /> During the {{ill|US occupation of Japan|ja|アメリカ軍占領期|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/US occupation of Japan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Minka exerted significant influence on academia and public discourse.<ref name="邱静" />
=== Transition from "Democratic Science" to "National Science" === Amid the surge of [[:en:Nationalism|nationalism]] across Asia after [[:en:World War II|World War II]], Minka adopted a new policy in its sixth congress (May 1951), declaring the need for "science that serves and learns from the people." By its seventh congress (May 1952), it aimed for the "creation and dissemination of national science."<ref name="村井1985" />
Ishimoto first introduced the term "national science" in January 1952, asserting the necessity of creating a science that could counter US oppression and contribute to {{ill|national liberation|ja|民族解放|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/national liberation|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="村井1985" />
==== National Science and the National Historical Science Movement ==== The National Historical Science Movement arose under the broader slogan of "creating and disseminating national science," emphasizing the need for historians to engage with the public and create a new type of historical science based on [[:en:Marxism|Marxism]].<ref name="yoshioka">[[佐々木毅]]他編『[[戦後史大辞典]] 増補縮刷版』[[:en:三省堂|三省堂]]、1995th year 6月、p.296</ref>
=== Decline of the Movement === From 1955 to 1956, Minka's History Division disbanded, and the movement's organizational basis collapsed.<ref name="村井1985" /> Internal disputes within the Japanese Communist Party and external criticism of the movement's methodology accelerated its decline.<ref name="oguma3">小熊 2002nd year p.331</ref>
== Legacy == The National Historical Science Movement had a lasting impact on the study of history in Japan, particularly in highlighting the importance of involving ordinary people in historical narrative construction.
=== Women's History === The focus on oral histories and the inclusion of women in historical narratives laid the groundwork for the emergence of [[:en:Women's history|women's history]] as a new field of study.<ref name="oohara">{{Cite journal|和書|author=倉敷伸子 |date=2007-11 |title=女性史研究とオーラル・ヒストリー |journal=大原社会問題研究所雑誌 |ISSN=09129421 |publisher=法政大学大原社会問題研究所 |issue=588 |pages=15-27 |id={{NDLJP|9972363}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/9972363/1/1}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Controversies === The movement has been both praised for its democratic ideals and criticized for its politicization and methodological weaknesses. Scholars such as [[:en:Eiji Oguma|Eiji Oguma]] have assessed the movement as a necessary yet flawed attempt to democratize historical science.<ref name="amino">『網野善彦対談集 「日本」をめぐって』、[[:en:講談社|講談社]]、2002nd year 1月、p.217 - 219</ref>

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q17228488]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''National Historical Science Movement''' (国民的歴史学運動, Kokuminteki Rekishigaku Undō) refers to a series of [[:en:Social movement|social movements]] undertaken in the field of {{ill|historical science|ja|歴史学|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/historical science|qq=|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} and {{ill|mass movements|ja|大衆運動|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mass movements|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 市民活動|1=civic activity|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17173040}} by the History Division of the {{ill|Democratic Scientists Association|ja|民主主義科学者協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Democratic Scientists Association|qq=|1=Association of Democratic Scientists|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547833}} (Minka) from the early to mid-{{ill|1950s|ja|1950th year 代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1950s|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="村井1985">{{Cite journal|和書|author=村井淳志 |date=1985-07 |title=国民的歴史学運動と歴史教育 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10748/2598 |journal=教育科学研究 |ISSN=0289-7121 |publisher=東京都立大学教育学研究室 |volume=4 |pages=23-32}}</ref> The movement was proposed by historian [[:en:Ishimoda Shō|Masao Ishimoto]], who was researching {{ill|Japanese medieval history|ja|日本中世史|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese medieval history|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中世|1=Middle Ages|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q12554}}, shortly after Minka declared its goal of creating and disseminating "national science" in [[:en:1952|1952]].<ref name="村井1985" /> Based on slogans like "revolution in historical science" and "making historical science belong to the people," the movement aligned with the armed struggle policy of the [[:en:Japanese Communist Party|Japanese Communist Party]], particularly the {{ill|Shokan faction|ja|所感派|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokan faction|qq=|1=Shokan sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11496759}}.<ref name="nation">小熊 2002nd year p.325</ref>
The blending of [[:en:Ethnic nationalism|nationalism]] and {{ill|politicism|ja|政治主義|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/politicism|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the movement sparked intense debate. Following the {{ill|Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|ja|日本共産党第6回全国協議会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|qq=|1=Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11507340}} in 1955, which marked a policy shift, and the dissolution of Minka's History Division between 1955 and 1956, the movement began to wane.<ref name="村井1985" /> Ishimoto himself submitted a self-critical essay in 1957, and the National Historical Science Movement eventually became regarded as a "nightmare of the past" in the field of historical science.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" /> Meanwhile, [[:en:Positivism|positivism]] based on written historical sources gained prominence in Japanese historical studies.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" />
== History == === Democratic Scientists Association === The Democratic Scientists Association (Minka) was established in [[:en:1946|1946]] with 180 members.<ref name="邱静">{{Cite thesis|和書 |author=邱静 |title=戦後における知識人の思想と政治 : 憲法問題研究会を中心に |volume=早稲田大学 |series=博士 (学術) 甲第2555号 |year=2008 |naid=500000435149 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2065/28633}}</ref> The association was critical of the prewar attitudes of scientists toward [[:en:Power (social and political)|authority]], the hierarchical structure of academic societies, and the lack of openness in science.<ref name="村井1985" />
Minka advocated for a new form of science, referred to comprehensively as "democratic science," and pursued research along these lines.<ref name="村井1985" /> By [[:en:1950|1950]], it had reached its peak with 114 local branches, 1,772 specialized members, and 8,243 ordinary members, including researchers, general citizens, and students.<ref name="邱静" /> During the {{ill|US occupation of Japan|ja|アメリカ軍占領期|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/US occupation of Japan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Minka exerted significant influence on academia and public discourse.<ref name="邱静" />
=== Transition from "Democratic Science" to "National Science" === Amid the surge of [[:en:Nationalism|nationalism]] across Asia after [[:en:World War II|World War II]], Minka adopted a new policy in its sixth congress (May 1951), declaring the need for "science that serves and learns from the people." By its seventh congress (May 1952), it aimed for the "creation and dissemination of national science."<ref name="村井1985" />
Ishimoto first introduced the term "national science" in January 1952, asserting the necessity of creating a science that could counter US oppression and contribute to {{ill|national liberation|ja|民族解放|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/national liberation|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="村井1985" />
==== National Science and the National Historical Science Movement ==== The National Historical Science Movement arose under the broader slogan of "creating and disseminating national science," emphasizing the need for historians to engage with the public and create a new type of historical science based on [[:en:Marxism|Marxism]].<ref name="yoshioka">[[佐々木毅]]他編『[[戦後史大辞典]] 増補縮刷版』[[:en:三省堂|三省堂]]、1995th year 6月、p.296</ref>
=== Decline of the Movement === From 1955 to 1956, Minka's History Division disbanded, and the movement's organizational basis collapsed.<ref name="村井1985" /> Internal disputes within the Japanese Communist Party and external criticism of the movement's methodology accelerated its decline.<ref name="oguma3">小熊 2002nd year p.331</ref>
== Legacy == The National Historical Science Movement had a lasting impact on the study of history in Japan, particularly in highlighting the importance of involving ordinary people in historical narrative construction.
=== Women's History === The focus on oral histories and the inclusion of women in historical narratives laid the groundwork for the emergence of [[:en:Women's history|women's history]] as a new field of study.<ref name="oohara">{{Cite journal|和書|author=倉敷伸子 |date=2007-11 |title=女性史研究とオーラル・ヒストリー |journal=大原社会問題研究所雑誌 |ISSN=09129421 |publisher=法政大学大原社会問題研究所 |issue=588 |pages=15-27 |id={{NDLJP|9972363}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/9972363/1/1}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Controversies === The movement has been both praised for its democratic ideals and criticized for its politicization and methodological weaknesses. Scholars such as [[:en:Eiji Oguma|Eiji Oguma]] have assessed the movement as a necessary yet flawed attempt to democratize historical science.<ref name="amino">『網野善彦対談集 「日本」をめぐって』、[[:en:講談社|講談社]]、2002nd year 1月、p.217 - 219</ref>

(UTC)}} '''National Historical Science Movement''' (国民的歴史学運動, Kokuminteki Rekishigaku Undō) refers to a series of [[:en:Social movement|social movements]] undertaken in the field of {{ill|historical science|ja|歴史学|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/historical science|qq=|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} and {{ill|mass movements|ja|大衆運動|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mass movements|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 市民活動|1=civic activity|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17173040}} by the History Division of the {{ill|Democratic Scientists Association|ja|民主主義科学者協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Democratic Scientists Association|qq=|1=Association of Democratic Scientists|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547833}} (Minka) from the early to mid-{{ill|1950s|ja|1950th year 代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1950s|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="村井1985">{{Cite journal|和書|author=村井淳志 |date=1985-07 |title=国民的歴史学運動と歴史教育 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10748/2598 |journal=教育科学研究 |ISSN=0289-7121 |publisher=東京都立大学教育学研究室 |volume=4 |pages=23-32}}</ref> The movement was proposed by historian [[:en:Ishimoda Shō|Masao Ishimoto]], who was researching {{ill|Japanese medieval history|ja|日本中世史|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese medieval history|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中世|1=Middle Ages|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q12554}}, shortly after Minka declared its goal of creating and disseminating "national science" in [[:en:1952|1952]].<ref name="村井1985" /> Based on slogans like "revolution in historical science" and "making historical science belong to the people," the movement aligned with the armed struggle policy of the [[:en:Japanese Communist Party|Japanese Communist Party]], particularly the {{ill|Shokan faction|ja|所感派|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokan faction|qq=|1=Shokan sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11496759}}.<ref name="nation">小熊 2002nd year p.325</ref>
The blending of [[:en:Ethnic nationalism|nationalism]] and {{ill|politicism|ja|政治主義|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/politicism|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the movement sparked intense debate. Following the {{ill|Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|ja|日本共産党第6回全国協議会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|qq=|1=Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11507340}} in 1955, which marked a policy shift, and the dissolution of Minka's History Division between 1955 and 1956, the movement began to wane.<ref name="村井1985" /> Ishimoto himself submitted a self-critical essay in 1957, and the National Historical Science Movement eventually became regarded as a "nightmare of the past" in the field of historical science.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" /> Meanwhile, [[:en:Positivism|positivism]] based on written historical sources gained prominence in Japanese historical studies.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" />
== History == === Democratic Scientists Association === The Democratic Scientists Association (Minka) was established in [[:en:1946|1946]] with 180 members.<ref name="邱静">{{Cite thesis|和書 |author=邱静 |title=戦後における知識人の思想と政治 : 憲法問題研究会を中心に |volume=早稲田大学 |series=博士 (学術) 甲第2555号 |year=2008 |naid=500000435149 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2065/28633}}</ref> The association was critical of the prewar attitudes of scientists toward [[:en:Power (social and political)|authority]], the hierarchical structure of academic societies, and the lack of openness in science.<ref name="村井1985" />
Minka advocated for a new form of science, referred to comprehensively as "democratic science," and pursued research along these lines.<ref name="村井1985" /> By [[:en:1950|1950]], it had reached its peak with 114 local branches, 1,772 specialized members, and 8,243 ordinary members, including researchers, general citizens, and students.<ref name="邱静" /> During the {{ill|US occupation of Japan|ja|アメリカ軍占領期|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/US occupation of Japan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Minka exerted significant influence on academia and public discourse.<ref name="邱静" />
=== Transition from "Democratic Science" to "National Science" === Amid the surge of [[:en:Nationalism|nationalism]] across Asia after [[:en:World War II|World War II]], Minka adopted a new policy in its sixth congress (May 1951), declaring the need for "science that serves and learns from the people." By its seventh congress (May 1952), it aimed for the "creation and dissemination of national science."<ref name="村井1985" />
Ishimoto first introduced the term "national science" in January 1952, asserting the necessity of creating a science that could counter US oppression and contribute to {{ill|national liberation|ja|民族解放|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/national liberation|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="村井1985" />
==== National Science and the National Historical Science Movement ==== The National Historical Science Movement arose under the broader slogan of "creating and disseminating national science," emphasizing the need for historians to engage with the public and create a new type of historical science based on [[:en:Marxism|Marxism]].<ref name="yoshioka">[[佐々木毅]]他編『[[戦後史大辞典]] 増補縮刷版』[[:en:三省堂|三省堂]]、1995th year 6月、p.296</ref>
=== Decline of the Movement === From 1955 to 1956, Minka's History Division disbanded, and the movement's organizational basis collapsed.<ref name="村井1985" /> Internal disputes within the Japanese Communist Party and external criticism of the movement's methodology accelerated its decline.<ref name="oguma3">小熊 2002nd year p.331</ref>
== Legacy == The National Historical Science Movement had a lasting impact on the study of history in Japan, particularly in highlighting the importance of involving ordinary people in historical narrative construction.
=== Women's History === The focus on oral histories and the inclusion of women in historical narratives laid the groundwork for the emergence of [[:en:Women's history|women's history]] as a new field of study.<ref name="oohara">{{Cite journal|和書|author=倉敷伸子 |date=2007-11 |title=女性史研究とオーラル・ヒストリー |journal=大原社会問題研究所雑誌 |ISSN=09129421 |publisher=法政大学大原社会問題研究所 |issue=588 |pages=15-27 |id={{NDLJP|9972363}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/9972363/1/1}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Controversies === The movement has been both praised for its democratic ideals and criticized for its politicization and methodological weaknesses. Scholars such as [[:en:Eiji Oguma|Eiji Oguma]] have assessed the movement as a necessary yet flawed attempt to democratize historical science.<ref name="amino">『網野善彦対談集 「日本」をめぐって』、[[:en:講談社|講談社]]、2002nd year 1月、p.217 - 219</ref>

[[:en:Ishimoda Shō|Masao Ishimoto]], who was researching {{ill|Japanese medieval history|ja|日本中世史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese medieval history|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中世|1=Middle Ages|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q12554}}, shortly after Minka declared its goal of creating and disseminating "national science" in [[:en:1952|1952]].<ref name="村井1985" /> Based on slogans like "revolution in historical science" and "making historical science belong to the people," the movement aligned with the armed struggle policy of the [[:en:Japanese Communist Party|Japanese Communist Party]], particularly the {{ill|Shokan faction|ja|所感派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokan faction|1=Shokan sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11496759}}.<ref name="nation">小熊 2002nd year p.325</ref>
The blending of [[:en:Ethnic nationalism|nationalism]] and {{ill|politicism|ja|政治主義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/politicism|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the movement sparked intense debate. Following the {{ill|Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|ja|日本共産党第6回全国協議会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|1=Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11507340}} in 1955, which marked a policy shift, and the dissolution of Minka's History Division between 1955 and 1956, the movement began to wane.<ref name="村井1985" /> Ishimoto himself submitted a self-critical essay in 1957, and the National Historical Science Movement eventually became regarded as a "nightmare of the past" in the field of historical science.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" /> Meanwhile, [[:en:Positivism|positivism]] based on written historical sources gained prominence in Japanese historical studies.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" />
== History == === Democratic Scientists Association === The Democratic Scientists Association (Minka) was established in [[:en:1946|1946]] with 180 members.<ref name="邱静">{{Cite thesis|和書 |author=邱静 |title=戦後における知識人の思想と政治 : 憲法問題研究会を中心に |volume=早稲田大学 |series=博士 (学術) 甲第2555号 |year=2008 |naid=500000435149 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2065/28633}}</ref> The association was critical of the prewar attitudes of scientists toward [[:en:Power (social and political)|authority]], the hierarchical structure of academic societies, and the lack of openness in science.<ref name="村井1985" />
Minka advocated for a new form of science, referred to comprehensively as "democratic science," and pursued research along these lines.<ref name="村井1985" /> By [[:en:1950|1950]], it had reached its peak with 114 local branches, 1,772 specialized members, and 8,243 ordinary members, including researchers, general citizens, and students.<ref name="邱静" /> During the {{ill|US occupation of Japan|ja|アメリカ軍占領期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/US occupation of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Minka exerted significant influence on academia and public discourse.<ref name="邱静" />
=== Transition from "Democratic Science" to "National Science" === Amid the surge of [[:en:Nationalism|nationalism]] across Asia after [[:en:World War II|World War II]], Minka adopted a new policy in its sixth congress (May 1951), declaring the need for "science that serves and learns from the people." By its seventh congress (May 1952), it aimed for the "creation and dissemination of national science."<ref name="村井1985" />
Ishimoto first introduced the term "national science" in January 1952, asserting the necessity of creating a science that could counter US oppression and contribute to {{ill|national liberation|ja|民族解放|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/national liberation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="村井1985" />
==== National Science and the National Historical Science Movement ==== The National Historical Science Movement arose under the broader slogan of "creating and disseminating national science," emphasizing the need for historians to engage with the public and create a new type of historical science based on [[:en:Marxism|Marxism]].<ref name="yoshioka">[[佐々木毅]]他編『[[戦後史大辞典]] 増補縮刷版』[[:en:三省堂|三省堂]]、1995th year 6月、p.296</ref>
=== Decline of the Movement === From 1955 to 1956, Minka's History Division disbanded, and the movement's organizational basis collapsed.<ref name="村井1985" /> Internal disputes within the Japanese Communist Party and external criticism of the movement's methodology accelerated its decline.<ref name="oguma3">小熊 2002nd year p.331</ref>
== Legacy == The National Historical Science Movement had a lasting impact on the study of history in Japan, particularly in highlighting the importance of involving ordinary people in historical narrative construction.
=== Women's History === The focus on oral histories and the inclusion of women in historical narratives laid the groundwork for the emergence of [[:en:Women's history|women's history]] as a new field of study.<ref name="oohara">{{Cite journal|和書|author=倉敷伸子 |date=2007-11 |title=女性史研究とオーラル・ヒストリー |journal=大原社会問題研究所雑誌 |ISSN=09129421 |publisher=法政大学大原社会問題研究所 |issue=588 |pages=15-27 |id={{NDLJP|9972363}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/9972363/1/1}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Controversies === The movement has been both praised for its democratic ideals and criticized for its politicization and methodological weaknesses. Scholars such as [[:en:Eiji Oguma|Eiji Oguma]] have assessed the movement as a necessary yet flawed attempt to democratize historical science.<ref name="amino">『網野善彦対談集 「日本」をめぐって』、[[:en:講談社|講談社]]、2002nd year 1月、p.217 - 219</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}

The blending of [[:en:Ethnic nationalism|nationalism]] and {{ill|politicism|ja|政治主義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/politicism|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the movement sparked intense debate. Following the {{ill|Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|ja|日本共産党第6回全国協議会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|1=Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11507340}} in 1955, which marked a policy shift, and the dissolution of Minka's History Division between 1955 and 1956, the movement began to wane.<ref name="村井1985" /> Ishimoto himself submitted a self-critical essay in 1957, and the National Historical Science Movement eventually became regarded as a "nightmare of the past" in the field of historical science.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" /> Meanwhile, [[:en:Positivism|positivism]] based on written historical sources gained prominence in Japanese historical studies.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" />
== History == === Democratic Scientists Association === The Democratic Scientists Association (Minka) was established in [[:en:1946|1946]] with 180 members.<ref name="邱静">{{Cite thesis|和書 |author=邱静 |title=戦後における知識人の思想と政治 : 憲法問題研究会を中心に |volume=早稲田大学 |series=博士 (学術) 甲第2555号 |year=2008 |naid=500000435149 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2065/28633}}</ref> The association was critical of the prewar attitudes of scientists toward [[:en:Power (social and political)|authority]], the hierarchical structure of academic societies, and the lack of openness in science.<ref name="村井1985" />
Minka advocated for a new form of science, referred to comprehensively as "democratic science," and pursued research along these lines.<ref name="村井1985" /> By [[:en:1950|1950]], it had reached its peak with 114 local branches, 1,772 specialized members, and 8,243 ordinary members, including researchers, general citizens, and students.<ref name="邱静" /> During the {{ill|US occupation of Japan|ja|アメリカ軍占領期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/US occupation of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Minka exerted significant influence on academia and public discourse.<ref name="邱静" />
=== Transition from "Democratic Science" to "National Science" === Amid the surge of [[:en:Nationalism|nationalism]] across Asia after [[:en:World War II|World War II]], Minka adopted a new policy in its sixth congress (May 1951), declaring the need for "science that serves and learns from the people." By its seventh congress (May 1952), it aimed for the "creation and dissemination of national science."<ref name="村井1985" />
Ishimoto first introduced the term "national science" in January 1952, asserting the necessity of creating a science that could counter US oppression and contribute to {{ill|national liberation|ja|民族解放|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/national liberation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="村井1985" />
==== National Science and the National Historical Science Movement ==== The National Historical Science Movement arose under the broader slogan of "creating and disseminating national science," emphasizing the need for historians to engage with the public and create a new type of historical science based on [[:en:Marxism|Marxism]].<ref name="yoshioka">[[佐々木毅]]他編『[[戦後史大辞典]] 増補縮刷版』[[:en:三省堂|三省堂]]、1995th year 6月、p.296</ref>
=== Decline of the Movement === From 1955 to 1956, Minka's History Division disbanded, and the movement's organizational basis collapsed.<ref name="村井1985" /> Internal disputes within the Japanese Communist Party and external criticism of the movement's methodology accelerated its decline.<ref name="oguma3">小熊 2002nd year p.331</ref>
== Legacy == The National Historical Science Movement had a lasting impact on the study of history in Japan, particularly in highlighting the importance of involving ordinary people in historical narrative construction.
=== Women's History === The focus on oral histories and the inclusion of women in historical narratives laid the groundwork for the emergence of [[:en:Women's history|women's history]] as a new field of study.<ref name="oohara">{{Cite journal|和書|author=倉敷伸子 |date=2007-11 |title=女性史研究とオーラル・ヒストリー |journal=大原社会問題研究所雑誌 |ISSN=09129421 |publisher=法政大学大原社会問題研究所 |issue=588 |pages=15-27 |id={{NDLJP|9972363}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/9972363/1/1}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Controversies === The movement has been both praised for its democratic ideals and criticized for its politicization and methodological weaknesses. Scholars such as [[:en:Eiji Oguma|Eiji Oguma]] have assessed the movement as a necessary yet flawed attempt to democratize historical science.<ref name="amino">『網野善彦対談集 「日本」をめぐって』、[[:en:講談社|講談社]]、2002nd year 1月、p.217 - 219</ref>

The blending of [[:en:Ethnic nationalism|nationalism]] and {{ill|politicism|ja|政治主義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/politicism|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the movement sparked intense debate. Following the {{ill|Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|ja|日本共産党第6回全国協議会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|1=Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11507340}} in 1955, which marked a policy shift, and the dissolution of Minka's History Division between 1955 and 1956, the movement began to wane.<ref name="村井1985" /> Ishimoto himself submitted a self-critical essay in 1957, and the National Historical Science Movement eventually became regarded as a "nightmare of the past" in the field of historical science.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" /> Meanwhile, [[:en:Positivism|positivism]] based on written historical sources gained prominence in Japanese historical studies.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" />
== History == === Democratic Scientists Association === The Democratic Scientists Association (Minka) was established in [[:en:1946|1946]] with 180 members.<ref name="邱静">{{Cite thesis|和書 |author=邱静 |title=戦後における知識人の思想と政治 : 憲法問題研究会を中心に |volume=早稲田大学 |series=博士 (学術) 甲第2555号 |year=2008 |naid=500000435149 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2065/28633}}</ref> The association was critical of the prewar attitudes of scientists toward [[:en:Power (social and political)|authority]], the hierarchical structure of academic societies, and the lack of openness in science.<ref name="村井1985" />
Minka advocated for a new form of science, referred to comprehensively as "democratic science," and pursued research along these lines.<ref name="村井1985" /> By [[:en:1950|1950]], it had reached its peak with 114 local branches, 1,772 specialized members, and 8,243 ordinary members, including researchers, general citizens, and students.<ref name="邱静" /> During the {{ill|US occupation of Japan|ja|アメリカ軍占領期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/US occupation of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Minka exerted significant influence on academia and public discourse.<ref name="邱静" />
=== Transition from "Democratic Science" to "National Science" === Amid the surge of [[:en:Nationalism|nationalism]] across Asia after [[:en:World War II|World War II]], Minka adopted a new policy in its sixth congress (May 1951), declaring the need for "science that serves and learns from the people." By its seventh congress (May 1952), it aimed for the "creation and dissemination of national science."<ref name="村井1985" />
Ishimoto first introduced the term "national science" in January 1952, asserting the necessity of creating a science that could counter US oppression and contribute to {{ill|national liberation|ja|民族解放|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/national liberation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="村井1985" />
==== National Science and the National Historical Science Movement ==== The National Historical Science Movement arose under the broader slogan of "creating and disseminating national science," emphasizing the need for historians to engage with the public and create a new type of historical science based on [[:en:Marxism|Marxism]].<ref name="yoshioka">[[佐々木毅]]他編『[[戦後史大辞典]] 増補縮刷版』[[:en:三省堂|三省堂]]、1995th year 6月、p.296</ref>
=== Decline of the Movement === From 1955 to 1956, Minka's History Division disbanded, and the movement's organizational basis collapsed.<ref name="村井1985" /> Internal disputes within the Japanese Communist Party and external criticism of the movement's methodology accelerated its decline.<ref name="oguma3">小熊 2002nd year p.331</ref>
== Legacy == The National Historical Science Movement had a lasting impact on the study of history in Japan, particularly in highlighting the importance of involving ordinary people in historical narrative construction.
=== Women's History === The focus on oral histories and the inclusion of women in historical narratives laid the groundwork for the emergence of [[:en:Women's history|women's history]] as a new field of study.<ref name="oohara">{{Cite journal|和書|author=倉敷伸子 |date=2007-11 |title=女性史研究とオーラル・ヒストリー |journal=大原社会問題研究所雑誌 |ISSN=09129421 |publisher=法政大学大原社会問題研究所 |issue=588 |pages=15-27 |id={{NDLJP|9972363}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/9972363/1/1}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Controversies === The movement has been both praised for its democratic ideals and criticized for its politicization and methodological weaknesses. Scholars such as [[:en:Eiji Oguma|Eiji Oguma]] have assessed the movement as a necessary yet flawed attempt to democratize historical science.<ref name="amino">『網野善彦対談集 「日本」をめぐって』、[[:en:講談社|講談社]]、2002nd year 1月、p.217 - 219</ref>

The blending of [[:en:Ethnic nationalism|nationalism]] and {{ill|politicism|ja|政治主義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/politicism|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the movement sparked intense debate. Following the {{ill|Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|ja|日本共産党第6回全国協議会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|1=Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11507340}} in 1955, which marked a policy shift, and the dissolution of Minka's History Division between 1955 and 1956, the movement began to wane.<ref name="村井1985" /> Ishimoto himself submitted a self-critical essay in 1957, and the National Historical Science Movement eventually became regarded as a "nightmare of the past" in the field of historical science.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" /> Meanwhile, [[:en:Positivism|positivism]] based on written historical sources gained prominence in Japanese historical studies.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" />
== History == === Democratic Scientists Association === The Democratic Scientists Association (Minka) was established in [[:en:1946|1946]] with 180 members.<ref name="邱静">{{Cite thesis|和書 |author=邱静 |title=戦後における知識人の思想と政治 : 憲法問題研究会を中心に |volume=早稲田大学 |series=博士 (学術) 甲第2555号 |year=2008 |naid=500000435149 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2065/28633}}</ref> The association was critical of the prewar attitudes of scientists toward [[:en:Power (social and political)|authority]], the hierarchical structure of academic societies, and the lack of openness in science.<ref name="村井1985" />
Minka advocated for a new form of science, referred to comprehensively as "democratic science," and pursued research along these lines.<ref name="村井1985" /> By [[:en:1950|1950]], it had reached its peak with 114 local branches, 1,772 specialized members, and 8,243 ordinary members, including researchers, general citizens, and students.<ref name="邱静" /> During the {{ill|US occupation of Japan|ja|アメリカ軍占領期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/US occupation of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Minka exerted significant influence on academia and public discourse.<ref name="邱静" />
=== Transition from "Democratic Science" to "National Science" === Amid the surge of [[:en:Nationalism|nationalism]] across Asia after [[:en:World War II|World War II]], Minka adopted a new policy in its sixth congress (May 1951), declaring the need for "science that serves and learns from the people." By its seventh congress (May 1952), it aimed for the "creation and dissemination of national science."<ref name="村井1985" />
Ishimoto first introduced the term "national science" in January 1952, asserting the necessity of creating a science that could counter US oppression and contribute to {{ill|national liberation|ja|民族解放|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/national liberation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="村井1985" />
==== National Science and the National Historical Science Movement ==== The National Historical Science Movement arose under the broader slogan of "creating and disseminating national science," emphasizing the need for historians to engage with the public and create a new type of historical science based on [[:en:Marxism|Marxism]].<ref name="yoshioka">[[佐々木毅]]他編『[[戦後史大辞典]] 増補縮刷版』[[:en:三省堂|三省堂]]、1995th year 6月、p.296</ref>
=== Decline of the Movement === From 1955 to 1956, Minka's History Division disbanded, and the movement's organizational basis collapsed.<ref name="村井1985" /> Internal disputes within the Japanese Communist Party and external criticism of the movement's methodology accelerated its decline.<ref name="oguma3">小熊 2002nd year p.331</ref>
== Legacy == The National Historical Science Movement had a lasting impact on the study of history in Japan, particularly in highlighting the importance of involving ordinary people in historical narrative construction.
=== Women's History === The focus on oral histories and the inclusion of women in historical narratives laid the groundwork for the emergence of [[:en:Women's history|women's history]] as a new field of study.<ref name="oohara">{{Cite journal|和書|author=倉敷伸子 |date=2007-11 |title=女性史研究とオーラル・ヒストリー |journal=大原社会問題研究所雑誌 |ISSN=09129421 |publisher=法政大学大原社会問題研究所 |issue=588 |pages=15-27 |id={{NDLJP|9972363}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/9972363/1/1}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Controversies === The movement has been both praised for its democratic ideals and criticized for its politicization and methodological weaknesses. Scholars such as [[:en:Eiji Oguma|Eiji Oguma]] have assessed the movement as a necessary yet flawed attempt to democratize historical science.<ref name="amino">『網野善彦対談集 「日本」をめぐって』、[[:en:講談社|講談社]]、2002nd year 1月、p.217 - 219</ref>

proposed by historian [[:en:Ishimoda Shō|Masao Ishimoto]], who was researching {{ill|Japanese medieval history|ja|日本中世史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese medieval history|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中世|1=Middle Ages|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q12554}}, shortly after Minka declared its goal of creating and disseminating "national science" in [[:en:1952|1952]].<ref name="村井1985" /> Based on slogans like "revolution in historical science" and "making historical science belong to the people," the movement aligned with the armed struggle policy of the Following the {{ill|Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|ja|日本共産党第6回全国協議会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|1=Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11507340}} in 1955, which marked a policy shift, and the dissolution of Minka's History Division between 1955 and 1956, the movement began to wane.<ref name="村井1985" /> Ishimoto himself submitted a self-critical essay in 1957, and the National Historical Science Movement eventually became regarded as a "nightmare of the past" in the field of historical science.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" /> Meanwhile, [[:en:Positivism|positivism]] based on written historical sources gained prominence in Japanese historical studies.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" />
== History == === Democratic Scientists Association === The Democratic Scientists Association (Minka) was established in [[:en:1946|1946]] with 180 members.<ref name="邱静">{{Cite thesis|和書 |author=邱静 |title=戦後における知識人の思想と政治 : 憲法問題研究会を中心に |volume=早稲田大学 |series=博士 (学術) 甲第2555号 |year=2008 |naid=500000435149 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2065/28633}}</ref> The association was critical of the prewar attitudes of scientists toward [[:en:Power (social and political)|authority]], the hierarchical structure of academic societies, and the lack of openness in science.<ref name="村井1985" />
Minka advocated for a new form of science, referred to comprehensively as "democratic science," and pursued research along these lines.<ref name="村井1985" /> By [[:en:1950|1950]], it had reached its peak with 114 local branches, 1,772 specialized members, and 8,243 ordinary members, including researchers, general citizens, and students.<ref name="邱静" /> During the {{ill|US occupation of Japan|ja|アメリカ軍占領期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/US occupation of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Minka exerted significant influence on academia and public discourse.<ref name="邱静" />
=== Transition from "Democratic Science" to "National Science" === Amid the surge of [[:en:Nationalism|nationalism]] across Asia after [[:en:World War II|World War II]], Minka adopted a new policy in its sixth congress (May 1951), declaring the need for "science that serves and learns from the people." By its seventh congress (May 1952), it aimed for the "creation and dissemination of national science."<ref name="村井1985" />
Ishimoto first introduced the term "national science" in January 1952, asserting the necessity of creating a science that could counter US oppression and contribute to {{ill|national liberation|ja|民族解放|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/national liberation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="村井1985" />
==== National Science and the National Historical Science Movement ==== The National Historical Science Movement arose under the broader slogan of "creating and disseminating national science," emphasizing the need for historians to engage with the public and create a new type of historical science based on [[:en:Marxism|Marxism]].<ref name="yoshioka">[[佐々木毅]]他編『[[戦後史大辞典]] 増補縮刷版』[[:en:三省堂|三省堂]]、1995th year 6月、p.296</ref>
=== Decline of the Movement === From 1955 to 1956, Minka's History Division disbanded, and the movement's organizational basis collapsed.<ref name="村井1985" /> Internal disputes within the Japanese Communist Party and external criticism of the movement's methodology accelerated its decline.<ref name="oguma3">小熊 2002nd year p.331</ref>
== Legacy == The National Historical Science Movement had a lasting impact on the study of history in Japan, particularly in highlighting the importance of involving ordinary people in historical narrative construction.
=== Women's History === The focus on oral histories and the inclusion of women in historical narratives laid the groundwork for the emergence of [[:en:Women's history|women's history]] as a new field of study.<ref name="oohara">{{Cite journal|和書|author=倉敷伸子 |date=2007-11 |title=女性史研究とオーラル・ヒストリー |journal=大原社会問題研究所雑誌 |ISSN=09129421 |publisher=法政大学大原社会問題研究所 |issue=588 |pages=15-27 |id={{NDLJP|9972363}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/9972363/1/1}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Controversies === The movement has been both praised for its democratic ideals and criticized for its politicization and methodological weaknesses. Scholars such as [[:en:Eiji Oguma|Eiji Oguma]] have assessed the movement as a necessary yet flawed attempt to democratize historical science.<ref name="amino">『網野善彦対談集 「日本」をめぐって』、[[:en:講談社|講談社]]、2002nd year 1月、p.217 - 219</ref>

Following the {{ill|Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|ja|日本共産党第6回全国協議会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|1=Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11507340}} in 1955, which marked a policy shift, and the dissolution of Minka's History Division between 1955 and 1956, the movement began to wane.<ref name="村井1985" /> Ishimoto himself submitted a self-critical essay in 1957, and the National Historical Science Movement eventually became regarded as a "nightmare of the past" in the field of historical science.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" /> Meanwhile, [[:en:Positivism|positivism]] based on written historical sources gained prominence in Japanese historical studies.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" />
== History == === Democratic Scientists Association === The Democratic Scientists Association (Minka) was established in [[:en:1946|1946]] with 180 members.<ref name="邱静">{{Cite thesis|和書 |author=邱静 |title=戦後における知識人の思想と政治 : 憲法問題研究会を中心に |volume=早稲田大学 |series=博士 (学術) 甲第2555号 |year=2008 |naid=500000435149 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2065/28633}}</ref> The association was critical of the prewar attitudes of scientists toward [[:en:Power (social and political)|authority]], the hierarchical structure of academic societies, and the lack of openness in science.<ref name="村井1985" />
Minka advocated for a new form of science, referred to comprehensively as "democratic science," and pursued research along these lines.<ref name="村井1985" /> By [[:en:1950|1950]], it had reached its peak with 114 local branches, 1,772 specialized members, and 8,243 ordinary members, including researchers, general citizens, and students.<ref name="邱静" /> During the {{ill|US occupation of Japan|ja|アメリカ軍占領期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/US occupation of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Minka exerted significant influence on academia and public discourse.<ref name="邱静" />
=== Transition from "Democratic Science" to "National Science" === Amid the surge of [[:en:Nationalism|nationalism]] across Asia after [[:en:World War II|World War II]], Minka adopted a new policy in its sixth congress (May 1951), declaring the need for "science that serves and learns from the people." By its seventh congress (May 1952), it aimed for the "creation and dissemination of national science."<ref name="村井1985" />
Ishimoto first introduced the term "national science" in January 1952, asserting the necessity of creating a science that could counter US oppression and contribute to {{ill|national liberation|ja|民族解放|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/national liberation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="村井1985" />
==== National Science and the National Historical Science Movement ==== The National Historical Science Movement arose under the broader slogan of "creating and disseminating national science," emphasizing the need for historians to engage with the public and create a new type of historical science based on [[:en:Marxism|Marxism]].<ref name="yoshioka">[[佐々木毅]]他編『[[戦後史大辞典]] 増補縮刷版』[[:en:三省堂|三省堂]]、1995th year 6月、p.296</ref>
=== Decline of the Movement === From 1955 to 1956, Minka's History Division disbanded, and the movement's organizational basis collapsed.<ref name="村井1985" /> Internal disputes within the Japanese Communist Party and external criticism of the movement's methodology accelerated its decline.<ref name="oguma3">小熊 2002nd year p.331</ref>
== Legacy == The National Historical Science Movement had a lasting impact on the study of history in Japan, particularly in highlighting the importance of involving ordinary people in historical narrative construction.
=== Women's History === The focus on oral histories and the inclusion of women in historical narratives laid the groundwork for the emergence of [[:en:Women's history|women's history]] as a new field of study.<ref name="oohara">{{Cite journal|和書|author=倉敷伸子 |date=2007-11 |title=女性史研究とオーラル・ヒストリー |journal=大原社会問題研究所雑誌 |ISSN=09129421 |publisher=法政大学大原社会問題研究所 |issue=588 |pages=15-27 |id={{NDLJP|9972363}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/9972363/1/1}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Controversies === The movement has been both praised for its democratic ideals and criticized for its politicization and methodological weaknesses. Scholars such as [[:en:Eiji Oguma|Eiji Oguma]] have assessed the movement as a necessary yet flawed attempt to democratize historical science.<ref name="amino">『網野善彦対談集 「日本」をめぐって』、[[:en:講談社|講談社]]、2002nd year 1月、p.217 - 219</ref>

代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1950s|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="村井1985">{{Cite journal|和書|author=村井淳志 |date=1985-07 |title=国民的歴史学運動と歴史教育 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10748/2598 |journal=教育科学研究 |ISSN=0289-7121 |publisher=東京都立大学教育学研究室 |volume=4 |pages=23-32}}</ref> The movement was proposed by historian {{ill|Ishimoda Shō|en|Ishimoda Shō|WD=Q6080349|lt=Masao Ishimoto}}, who was researching {{ill|Japanese medieval history|ja|日本中世史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese medieval history|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中世|1=Middle Ages|comment=changed title based on "revolution in historical science" and "making historical science belong to the people," the movement aligned with the armed struggle policy of the {{ill|Japanese Communist Party|en|Japanese Communist Party|WD=Q641600|lt=Japanese Communist Party}}, particularly the {{ill|Shokan faction|ja|所感派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokan faction|1=Shokan sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11496759}}.<ref name="nation">小熊 2002nd year p.325</ref>
The blending of {{ill|Ethnic nationalism|en|Ethnic nationalism|WD=Q637251|lt=nationalism}} and {{ill|politicism|ja|政治主義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/politicism|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the movement sparked intense debate. Following the {{ill|Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|ja|日本共産党第6回全国協議会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|1=Sixth National Congress of the Japanese Communist Party|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11507340}} in 1955, which marked a policy shift, and the dissolution of Minka's History Division between 1955 and 1956, the movement began to wane.<ref name="村井1985" /> Ishimoto himself submitted a self-critical essay in 1957, and the National Historical Science Movement eventually became regarded as a "nightmare of the past" in the field of historical science.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" /> Meanwhile, {{ill|Positivism|en|Positivism|WD=Q131015|lt=positivism}} based on written historical sources gained prominence in Japanese historical studies.<ref name="小熊英二|2002|p=351" />

Minka advocated for a new form of science, referred to comprehensively as "democratic science," and pursued research along these lines.<ref name="村井1985" /> By {{ill|1950|en|1950|WD=Q18597|lt=1950}}, it had reached its peak with 114 local branches, 1,772 specialized members, and 8,243 ordinary members, including researchers, general citizens, and students.<ref name="邱静" /> During the {{ill|US occupation of Japan|ja|アメリカ軍占領期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/US occupation of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Minka exerted significant influence on academia and public discourse.<ref name="邱静" />
